data.title: Ex-military leaders back Mark Kelly in lawsuit against Hegseth
data.description:
More than 40 former military leaders are backing Sen. Mark Kelly’s (D-Ariz.) lawsuit against the Pentagon, saying they are “deeply concerned” Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth’s threats against the retired naval officer could chill the speech of other former service members. 

The 41 individuals – which included retired four-star generals, admirals, and service secretaries who have served in both Republican and Democratic administrations – filed a brief in federal court on Tuesday in support of Kelly’s effort to end Hegseth’s administrative proceedings that could reduce his rank and pay.

“Disciplining Senator Kelly for engaging in protected, political speech violates the First Amendment and, if permitted to stand, would chill public participation by veterans around the country,” the group states in the 21-page brief filed in U.S. District Court for the District of Columbia.

“Diverse viewpoints are critical to a healthy and free marketplace of ideas, and silencing veteran voices would be especially harmful — depriving the public of experienced and informed views on critical matters of national security.”

The legal fight stems from a video Kelly filmed in the fall with five other Democratic members of Congress at the height of the controversy around the administration’s Caribbean boat strikes. In it, the lawmakers remind military members they are allowed to not carry out illegal orders but don’t specifically mention the strikes. 

Hegseth almost directly after the video was released announced an investigation into Kelly, then earlier this month said he was formally censuring him and initiating proceedings that could reduce his retired military rank and pension. 

Kelly, in turn, sued Hegseth in federal court to block the disciplinary action, arguing it violates his First Amendment rights and chills speech for veterans. The Pentagon chief is disciplining him “solely for the content and viewpoint of his political speech,” Kelly alleges, adding that Hegseth does not have a legal basis to open a review of his retirement grade.

“Nothing in the statute authorizes the Department of Defense to reopen that determination based on post-retirement political speech – and if it did, it would raise serious constitutional concerns and subject all of the nation’s retired veterans to an ever-present threat against their retirement,” according to the case.

The former astronaut and Navy pilot who flew 39 combat missions during Operation Desert Storm in Iraq has staunchly refused to back down to the administration, a stance that has elevated his national profile.

In Tuesday’s legal filing, the group notes that they are aware the administration could attempt to punish them for speaking out against it, but did so “in light of the gravity of the issues in this case and the potential implications for millions of active-duty and retired members of the armed forces.”

“This decision is not made lightly: the attempt to punish Senator Kelly suggests that public expressions of disagreement with the Secretary – even if made in good faith and supported factually – invite possible retaliation,” according to the brief.

It also said the group was “aware of many fellow veterans who would eagerly participate in public debate on important and contested issues, but are declining to do so today, fearing official reprisal.” 

An in-person injunction hearing on the lawsuit is set for Jan. 28.

data.title: Israel begins bulldozing UNRWA Jerusalem headquarters
data.description:
Israeli authorities on Tuesday started bulldozing United Nations Relief and Works Agency (UNRWA) offices in Jerusalem’s Sheikh Jarrah neighborhood, part of the country’s crackdown on one of the global agencies focused on delivering humanitarian aid to Palestinians.

U.N. Secretary-General António Guterres slammed the demolition and called for it to be returned to U.N. control.

“The Secretary-General views as wholly unacceptable the continued escalatory actions against UNRWA, which are inconsistent with Israel’s clear obligations under international law,” Farhan Haq, the deputy U.N. spokesperson, said.

“What we saw today is the culmination of two years of incitement and measures against UNRWA in east Jerusalem,” Roland Friedrich, the director of UNRWA in the West Bank, said.

Staff have not used the east Jerusalem headquarters for nearly a year due to safety concerns. Friedrich added that Israeli forces fired tear gas at a vocational school where over 300 young refugees receive job training there. One of them, a 15-year-old, was hit in the eye with a rubber bullet, according to the Palestinian Authority’s Jerusalem governorate.

Israel has accused UNRWA of being infiltrated by Hamas and claimed some of its employees were part of the Oct. 7, 2021, attack in Israel that led to its military deployment across the Gaza Strip. UNRWA has denied these allegations and took action against employees accused of being part of the attack.

The Knesset passed legislation in late 2024 to ban the agency in Israel, which led to the country pulling out of its 1967 agreement with UNRWA. The U.S. paused its funding for the agency over the allegations made about UNRWA staff being part of the Oct. 7 attack.

National Security Minister Itamar Ben Gvir praised the demolition as an Israeli flag was raised above the facility, calling it “a historic day.”

The demolition comes days after President Trump announced his Board of Peace to oversee Gaza, as part of his 20-point peace plan following Israel and Hamas’s fragile ceasefire went into effect late last year. Trump has sent invitations to several world leaders to be part of the board, with Argentinian President Javier Milei accepting his.

Trump also invited Russian President Vladimir Putin and leaders from the United Kingdom, Israel, Canada, France, Brazil, Egypt and Turkey. French President Emmanuel Macron’s office said he does not intend to join “at this stage.” Trump dismissed this, adding that “nobody wants him anyway because he’s going to be out of office very soon.”

Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu has also not publicly accepted his invitation and has been critical of the president’s board, in which Trump will serve as chairman. Netanyahu previously said the board was out of step with Israel and “contrary to its policy.”

The Associated Press contributed.

data.title: House Republican squares off against Nvidia, Sacks over AI chip bill
data.description:
Rep. Brian Mast (R-Fla.) is squaring off against chipmaker Nvidia and White House artificial intelligence (AI) and crypto czar David Sacks, as he seeks to defend his legislation that would give Congress the power to block certain AI chip exports.

Mast, who serves as chair of the House Foreign Affairs Committee, began fielding pushback to his AI Overwatch Act from several conservative accounts on the social platform X last week, including far-right activist Laura Loomer.

The AI Overwatch Act, which the congressman introduced in December, would allow Congress to block advanced chip exports to U.S. foreign adversaries like China and Russia, creating a review process modeled after Congress’ oversight of foreign arms sales.

Loomer slammed the measure Thursday as “pro-China sabotage disguised as oversight” and called on lawmakers to “kill the bill.”

“It yanks control of advanced AI chip exports away from President Trump who is aggressively blocking CCP access to these chips, and instead hands veto power to Congress,” she wrote on X.

“When the Democrats take back the House in 2026, Hakeem Jeffries @RepJeffries could greenlight sales of these chips to China or delay Trump’s America First crackdown to help our adversaries,” Loomer added.

Mast pushed back on Loomer, suggesting this “isn’t a serious argument” and is instead “NVIDIA’s lobbying talking points to sell chips to China.”

Sacks also chimed in, agreeing with a post accusing the bill of attempting to take away President Trump’s authorities and limit his ability to strategically position the U.S. in relation to China.

In response, Mast touted the president’s previous decisions to block sales of certain chipmaking tools and Nvidia’s most advanced chips to China, saying he is “going to work to continue that momentum.”

“You can advise him to sell H200 chips to China if you want, I advise the opposite,” he added to Sacks. “That’s your prerogative.”

At the heart of this debate are Trump’s recent decisions on chip exports, including Nvidia’s H200 chips, which have split the president and the China hawks in his party.

Last summer, the president agreed to allow sales of Nvidia’s H20 chips to China in exchange for a 15 percent cut of revenue.

The move faced pushback from Democrats and GOP China hawks, who voiced concerns that the chips could boost Beijing’s AI capabilities at a key moment in the race to dominate the technology.

Trump announced in December that he would also allow sales of the more powerful H200 chips in exchange for a 25 percent cut, prompting the same concerns.

Last week, the administration published new regulations formalizing the shift and announced a 25 percent tariff on some advanced chips, including the H200.

Mast took aim at Nvidia over the weekend amid the backlash to his bill, accusing the company of “fighting to sell millions of advanced AI chips to Chinese military companies like Alibaba and Tencent.”

“I’m trying to stop that from happening. Jensen, if you ever feel like debating the actual facts, I’ll meet you anytime, anywhere,” he wrote, referring to Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang.

“And every so-called MAGA influencer being paid to push this garbage should be embarrassed,” the Florida Republican added, criticizing the company for its policy allowing transgender employees to use bathrooms that align with their gender identities.

The Hill has reached out to Nvidia for comment.

Several other lawmakers have put forward measures to rein in AI chip exports in recent months.

The GAIN AI Act, from Sens. Jim Banks (R-Ind.) and Elizabeth Warren (D-Mass.), sought to require chipmakers to give U.S. companies first priority on chips.

It passed the Senate in October as part of the National Defense Authorization Act (NDAA) but was ultimately stripped out of the bill in the face of White House resistance to the measure.

Sens. Pete Ricketts (R-Neb.) and Chris Coons (D-Del.) also introduced the SAFE Chips Act in December, seeking to lock in existing chip export restrictions for more than two years.

data.title: DeSantis loses CFP Championship bet to Indiana governor
data.description:
Indiana Gov. Mike Braun (R) will soon be getting stone crabs and key lime pie courtesy of Florida Gov. Ron DeSantis (R) after Indiana University defeated the University of Miami in Monday’s College Football Playoff National Championship.

DeSantis said at a press conference on nursing education that Braun promised pork tenderloin and sugar cream pie if the Hurricanes won, state delicacies that “I’m sure [are] good, don’t get me wrong.”

Now, though, DeSantis owes his Hoosier State counterpart stone crabs and key lime pie from Joe’s Stone Crab, a famous restaurant in Miami Beach, Fla., ranked as the best eating spot in Florida by Chef Standards last July.

“There’s a little bit of an imbalance there,” the governor quipped to laughs.

The Hoosiers capped off a perfect 16-0 season with a 27-21 win over the Hurricanes at Hard Rock Stadium in Miami Gardens, Fla. It marks a striking turnaround for a program, led by head coach Curt Cignetti and Heisman Trophy-winning quarterback Fernando Mendoza, that until November had the most losses in college football history.

“Congrats to Indiana on a perfect 16-0 season,” DeSantis wrote Monday on the social platform X. “They have the best player, the best coach, and ran the table like no other school has done in the expanded playoff era.”

“Given the program’s historic futility, this may be the best achievement in college football history.”

The Florida governor said minutes later that Mendoza’s fourth-quarter touchdown run, which put Indiana up by two scores, was a “gutsy call” by Cignetti and offensive coordinator Mike Shanahan and “an amazing play.”

Braun, who attended the game, wrote on X after it ended that Indiana “showed grit, discipline, and heart all season and tonight, they delivered for Hoosiers everywhere.” 

Now, he will receive some stone crabs and key lime pie from his fellow GOP governor, who joked Tuesday that he could drop the food off at Marco Island off the coast of Miami Beach since “half of Indiana [is there] this time of year.”

data.title: Judge refuses to block limits on lawmakers' access to ICE facilities
data.description:
A federal judge on Monday refused to temporarily block a newly reinstated Department of Homeland Security (DHS) policy that restricts lawmakers from making unannounced visits to Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) detention centers.

A group of Democratic lawmakers, led by Rep. Joe Neguse (Colo.), sued the Trump administration last week seeking to bar enforcement of a policy requiring them to give seven days’ notice before any visit, arguing it violated a court order that struck down a similar rule last month.

Judge Jia M. Cobb, a Biden appointee, did not rule on the merits of the case itself but said that the plaintiffs used the “wrong procedural vehicle” to challenge it, concluding that the previous order did not apply.

“The Court emphasizes that it denies Plaintiffs’ motion only because it is not the proper avenue to challenge Defendants’ January 8, 2026 memorandum and the policy stated therein, rather than based on any kind of finding that the policy is lawful,” Cobb wrote in the four-page order.

The lawsuit came after three Minnesota lawmakers were reportedly denied access to an ICE processing center in Minneapolis earlier this month, days after 37-year-old Renee Good was fatally shot in her car during an altercation with a federal immigration officer.

The complaint stated lawmakers were denied entry under the policy, which they learned had been reinstated only after arriving on-site. They called the move a “transparent attempt by DHS to once again subvert Congress’s will.”

Congress inserted language into an appropriations bill in 2020 prohibiting DHS from using funds to “prevent” members of Congress from “entering, for the purpose of conducting oversight,” any DHS facility “used to detain or otherwise house aliens.”

Cobb cited that provision in a December decision, in which she determined that lawmakers had a right to make unannounced visits to detention centers.

Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem asserted in a Jan. 8 memo that the new policy was necessary “to ensure adequate protection for Members of Congress, congressional staff, detainees, and ICE employees alike.”

Noem further argued it would not be subject to Cobb’s previous order because it had determined ICE field offices, including holding facilities, were distinct from detention facilities.

“Unannounced visits require pulling ICE officers away from their normal duties,” Noem wrote. “Moreover, there is an increasing trend of replacing legitimate oversight activities with circus-like publicity stunts, all which creates a chaotic environment with heightened emotions.”

Cobb agreed the new policy “differs facially” from the one announced in June, and therefore, the previous stay does not extend.

Democracy Forward, which filed the lawsuit on behalf of Neguse and others, said in a statement Tuesday that it was exploring all legal options.

“We are reviewing the court’s order,” the statement said. “We will continue to use every legal tool available to stop the administration’s efforts to hide from congressional oversight.”

data.title: Trump is in hot water — just 6 percent satisfied with Epstein files handling
data.description:
At this point, the Jeffrey Epstein story isn’t just about a disgraced financier or a sealed set of files — it’s about trust. And right now, most Americans don’t have it.  

A new CNN poll makes that painfully clear. Only 6 percent of Americans say they’re satisfied with how the Trump administration has handled the release of Epstein-related documents. Six.  And nearly half of Americans, 49 percent, say they’re dissatisfied with how the administration has handled the release.   

Perhaps the most telling part is this: 67 percent of Americans believe the government is intentionally holding back information that should be public.  

That’s not partisan noise. That’s a credibility problem.  

Because despite the Justice Department releasing thousands of documents under pressure from Congress, officials admit those files represent less than 1 percent of what they actually have. Less than 1 percent. Meanwhile, the DOJ has acknowledged potentially more than a million additional Epstein-related documents still under review, with no clear timeline for when — or if — the public will see them at all.  

Congress didn’t politely request transparency, it mandated it. A bill passed overwhelmingly in the House and unanimously in the Senate required the full release of Epstein documents by mid-December. Not only has that deadline come and gone, but the administration has also failed to explain why so many files remain redacted, another requirement written directly into the law.  

And that’s where frustration turns into suspicion.  

President Trump has grown increasingly irritated by the focus on Epstein, particularly as midterm elections loom. But political annoyance doesn’t override public accountability. Especially when the documents that have been released include photos of Trump alongside Epstein — images the president says reflect a social relationship he ended years ago, which he maintains involved no wrongdoing.  

Former President Bill Clinton also appears in multiple photos, and his team has accused the administration of selectively highlighting those images. Again, there are no allegations of wrongdoing, but the selective transparency only fuels the perception that this process isn’t neutral. 

And perception matters. Because while the administration insists more attorneys are reviewing files — FYI, there are roughly 500 people now involved — Americans are still being asked to wait.  

Meanwhile, outside Washington’s marble halls, the public conversation isn’t slowing down. A group calling itself The Secret Handshake put up a massive installation on the National Mall — a 10-foot-tall replica of a birthday card allegedly sent to Epstein bearing Trump’s name, featuring a crude sketch and the line, “Happy Birthday — and may every day be another wonderful secret.” Trump denies writing it and has sued The Wall Street Journal over the report. But the symbolism is hard to miss.  

This isn’t about art or spectacle. It’s about a government that promised transparency and delivered delays. When Americans across party lines believe information is being withheld — not protected, not responsibly reviewed, but intentionally held back — that erodes confidence in institutions already hanging by a thread.  

Lindsey Granger is a NewsNation contributor and co-host of The Hill’s commentary show “Rising.” This column is an edited transcription of her on-air commentary.

data.title: Lawler slams Putin invite to Trump's Board of Peace for Gaza
data.description:
Rep. Mike Lawler (R-N.Y.) broke with President Trump over his decision to invite Russian President Vladimir Putin to serve on the Board of Peace for Gaza.

In an interview Tuesday on CNN’s “The Situation Room,” the moderate Republican slammed Putin as a “war criminal” and called for secondary sanctions to further isolate the Kremlin on the world stage amid the war in Ukraine.

“No,” Lawler said, when asked whether Putin is worthy of serving on the proposed board. “Vladimir Putin is a war criminal and a dictator and despot.”

“I believe we need to enact secondary sanctions on Russia and those that do business with Russia,” he continued. “And I think, frankly, Europe needs to step up to the plate and cut off Russian gas.”

“The fact is, Vladimir Putin continues to act in an aggressive posture in Ukraine and, you know, his continued unwillingness to actually negotiate a ceasefire, from my vantage point, requires Congress to act,” the New York Republican added. “And I think it‘s time to pass the sanctions legislation.”

Kremlin spokesperson Dmitry Peskov said Monday that Putin received an invitation “through diplomatic channels to join the Peace Council,” according to state-run news outlet RIA Novosti.

“We are studying all the details of this proposal, including hoping to contact the American side to clarify all the nuances,” Peskov added.

Trump confirmed the invitation, telling reporters, “Yes,” when asked if he invited the Russian leader to join the board formed to implement the U.S.-brokered peace plan between Israel and Hamas in Gaza.

“These are world leaders. The answer is yes, I did,” the president told reporters.

Trump is reportedly asking countries to pay $1 billion for membership on the board, with funds going toward rebuilding the Gaza Strip, much of which was destroyed in the conflict with Israel after Hamas’s Oct. 7, 2023, attack and the Israeli military’s subsequent strikes.

The United Kingdom, Canada, Egypt, Turkey, Brazil, Argentina and India are among the countries that have confirmed receipt of invitations to join the board. U.K. Prime Minister Keir Starmer said Monday the government was still discussing the terms.

The invitation to Putin comes almost four years into his war on Ukraine, which has killed some 15,000 Ukrainian civilians and hundreds of thousands of soldiers on both sides of the fight. Casualties have only escalated amid Trump’s push for peace since returning to power. 

The White House on Friday announced the founding members of the Board of Peace. The body, headed by Trump, will also include three of his closest diplomatic advisers — Secretary of State Marco Rubio, special envoy Steve Witkoff and the president’s son-in-law, Jared Kushner — along with former U.K. Prime Minister Tony Blair, World Bank President Ajay Banga, billionaire investors Marc Rowan and U.S. national security adviser Robert Gabriel.

Lawler, one of three House Republicans whose districts backed former Vice President Harris over Trump in 2024, has in the past broken with the president, including on issues related to foreign policy.

The lawmaker this past year opted to forgo an anticipated run for New York’s governor’s mansion and is running again for his seat in Congress.

data.title: Massie rips Trump's sale of Venezuelan oil 'for his own piggy bank'
data.description:
Rep. Thomas Massie (R-Ky.), criticized President Trump on Tuesday over his administration’s handling of Venezuela’s oil after ousting Venezuelan leader Nicolás Maduro earlier this month and the funds it will receive from the sales of that oil.

Massie, a fiscal hawk who has been known to break with GOP leaders on issues related to spending, described oil acquired from Venezuela as “stolen” in a post on social platform X and argued that the administration was circumventing Congress’s power of the purse.

“Selling stolen oil and putting billions of dollars in a bank in Qatar to be spent without Congressional approval is not Constitutional,” the lawmaker wrote. ”Only Congress can appropriate money.”

“The President can’t legally create a second Treasury overseas for his own piggy bank,” he added. “Wake up Congress.”

President Trump announced earlier this month that Venezuela would turn over between 30 million and 50 million barrels of oil to be sold by the U.S.

“This Oil will be sold at its Market Price, and that money will be controlled by me, as President of the United States of America, to ensure it is used to benefit the people of Venezuela and the United States!” Trump wrote on Truth Social at the time.

His administration announced last week that it completed its first sale of the oil. Semafor first reported that the $500 million revenue from the sale was being held in bank accounts, including one in Qatar, that are controlled by the U.S. government.

It could be just the start of such sales, as the Energy Department has indicated that the U.S. will sell Venezuelan oil “indefinitely.” The department also said it was working with “the world’s leading commodity marketers and key banks to execute and provide financial support for these crude oil and crude products sales.”

data.title: DOJ subpoenas Walz, Frey in immigration enforcement obstruction investigation
data.description:
The Department of Justice on Tuesday issued subpoenas to Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz (D) and Minneapolis Mayor Jacob Frey (D) for obstructing an immigration enforcement investigation.

Walz and Frey have been at the center of city and state opposition in Minneapolis to actions by the Trump administration to deport immigrants. It has set off demonstrations and protests across the city that have gone up in temperature since an Immigration and Customs Enforcement officer shot and killed a woman in the city.

Walz and Frey, who have both decried the killing of Renee Good, each confirmed they had been subpoenaed

“This Justice Department investigation, sparked by calls for accountability in the face of violence, chaos, and the killing of Renee Good, does not seek justice. It is a partisan distraction,” Walz wrote in a statement on X.

“Minnesotans are more concerned with safety and rather than with baseless legal tactics aimed at intimidating public servants standing shoulder to shoulder with their community. My focus has always been protecting the people of this state, not protecting myself,” he added.

Frey echoed similar concerns about the probe after being asked to turn over documents. The subpoenas referenced a statute used against the Oath Keepers and the Proud Boys after the Capitol insurrection, citing a conspiracy to impede federal officers from discharging their duties, sources told CBS News. 

“When the federal government weaponizes its power to try to intimidate local leaders for doing their jobs, every American should be concerned. We shouldn’t have to live in a country where people fear that federal law enforcement will be used to play politics or crack down on local voices they disagree with,” Frey said in a statement to NewsNation. 

“In Minneapolis, we won’t be afraid. We know the difference between right and wrong and, as Mayor, I’ll continue doing the job I was elected to do: keeping our community safe and standing up for our values,” he added.

Alongside Walz and Frey, Attorney General Keith Ellison and Hennepin County attorney Mary Moriarty were served at their offices.

data.title: Carney: Canada 'strongly opposes' tariffs over Greenland
data.description:
Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney on Tuesday said Canada “strongly opposes” President Trump’s 10 percent tariffs on Denmark and other European allies over his calls for the U.S. to acquire Greenland.

“Canada strongly opposes tariffs over Greenland and calls for focused talks to achieve our shared objectives of security and prosperity in the Arctic,” Carney said from Davos, Switzerland, at the World Economic Forum. “On plurilateral trade, we’re championing efforts to build a bridge between the trans-Pacific partnership and the European Union, which would create a new trading block of 1.5 billion people.”

“On Arctic sovereignty, we stand firmly with Greenland in Denmark and fully support their unique right to determine Greenland’s future,” he later said, met with applause. “Our commitment to NATO’s Article 5 is unwavering, so we’re working with our NATO allies, including the Nordic-Baltic Eight to further secure the alliance’s northern and western flanks, including through Canada’s unprecedented investments in over-the-horizon radar, in submarines and aircraft and boots on the ground — boots on the ice.”

Carney said Canada was forming “buyers’ clubs anchored in the G7 so that world can diversify away from concentrated supply.”

“And on AI, we’re cooperating with like-minded democracies to ensure that we won’t ultimately be forced to choose between hegemons and hyperscalers,” Carney added.

He said this work builds coalitions “that work, issue by issue, with partners who share enough common ground to act together. … What it’s doing is creating a dense web of connection across trade.”

“Argue that middle powers must act together, because if we’re not at the table, we’re on the menu,” Carney said.

President of the European Commission Ursula von der Leyen, who also spoke at Davos, called the new tariffs a mistake.

“Artic security can only be achieved together, and this is why the proposed additional tariffs are a mistake, especially between long-standing allies,” she said. “The European Union and the United States have agreed to a trade deal last July. And in politics, as in business, a deal is a deal. And when friends shake hands, it must mean something.”

Trump announced on Saturday that tariffs will go into effect for Denmark, the United Kingdom, the Netherlands, Germany, Norway, Sweden, France and Finland on Feb. 1. The levies are then expected to increase to 25 percent on June 1.

“This Tariff will be due and payable until such time as a Deal is reached for the Complete and Total purchase of Greenland,” Trump wrote on Truth Social.

The tariff announcement put a halt to a trade deal between the U.S. and European Union agreed to by von der Leyen and Trump last July. The deal would put 15 percent tariffs on European imports, with the EU agreeing to purchase $750 billion worth of energy from the U.S. and invest an additional $600 billion for other U.S. goods.

European Parliament lawmakers could retaliate against the U.S. over the tariffs, which Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent warned against on Tuesday. While speaking to Fox Business’s Maria Bartiromo from Davos, he urged European leaders to “sit back [and] take a deep breath.”

“Everyone, take a deep breath,” he said on CNBC on Tuesday. “Do not escalate, do not escalate. And President Trump has a strategy here. Hear him out, and then everything will be fine.”
